clean_story:
Storms vanishing. Deserts to blame.

The phrase "deserts stealing storms" describes how expanding drylands disrupt rain patterns—like a sponge soaking up water before it reaches the bucket.

Deserts don’t just sit still. Their heat rises, creating high-pressure zones that act like invisible domes, pushing storm clouds away. The Sahara does this aggressively: its hot air bulldozes moist Atlantic winds southward, starving the Sahel of rain. Satellite data shows storms detouring hundreds of kilometers around these "heat shields."

Worse, desert dust rides the wind. Tiny particles from the Gobi Desert, for example, seed clouds but also block sunlight, cooling the air below and weakening storm formation. In 2020, a record 24 million tons of Saharan dust crossed the Atlantic, smothering hurricanes before they could grow.

Three related subjects are dust’s climate role, monsoon shifts over time, and how cities alter wind.
